They are also more fuel-efficient in most circumstances and are used in heavy road vehicles, some automobiles (increasingly so for their increased [fuel efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency") over gasoline engines), ships, [railway](/wiki/Railway "Railway") [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive"), and light [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft"). Gasoline engines are used in most other road vehicles including most cars, [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle"), and [mopeds](/wiki/Moped "Moped"). In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), sophisticated diesel-engined cars have taken over about 45% of the market since the 1990s. There are also engines that run on [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen_car "Hydrogen car"), [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol"), [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") (LPG), [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel"), [paraffin](/wiki/Wood "Wood") and [tractor vaporizing oil](/wiki/Tractor_vaporizing_oil "Tractor vaporizing oil") (TVO).

#### Hydrogen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Hydrogen")]

Main article: [Hydrogen internal combustion engine vehicle](/wiki/Hydrogen_internal_combustion_engine_vehicle "Hydrogen internal combustion engine vehicle")

Hydrogen could eventually [replace conventional fossil fuels](/wiki/Hydrogen_economy "Hydrogen economy") in traditional internal combustion engines. Alternatively [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") technology may come to deliver its promise and the use of the internal combustion engines could even be phased out.

Although there are multiple ways of producing free hydrogen, those methods require converting combustible molecules into hydrogen or consuming electric energy. Unless that electricity is produced from a renewable source—and is not required for other purposes—hydrogen does not solve any [energy crisis](/wiki/Energy_crisis "Energy crisis"). In many situations the disadvantage of hydrogen, relative to carbon fuels, is [its storage](/wiki/Hydrogen_economy#Storage "Hydrogen economy"). [Liquid hydrogen](/wiki/Liquid_hydrogen "Liquid hydrogen") has extremely low density (14 times lower than water) and requires extensive insulation—whilst gaseous hydrogen requires heavy tankage. Even when liquefied, hydrogen has a higher [specific energy](/wiki/Specific_energy "Specific energy") but the volumetric energetic storage is still roughly five times lower than gasoline. However, the energy density of hydrogen is considerably higher than that of electric batteries, making it a serious contender as an energy carrier to replace fossil fuels. The 'Hydrogen on Demand' process (see [direct borohydride fuel cell](/wiki/Direct_borohydride_fuel_cell "Direct borohydride fuel cell")) creates hydrogen as needed, but has other issues, such as the high price of the [sodium borohydride](/wiki/Sodium_borohydride "Sodium borohydride") that is the raw material.

### Oxidizers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Oxidizers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Moore-single-cylinder-gasoline-engine.jpg/250px-Moore-single-cylinder-gasoline-engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moore-single-cylinder-gasoline-engine.jpg)

One-cylinder gasoline engine, c. 1910

Since air is plentiful at the surface of the earth, the oxidizer is typically atmospheric oxygen, which has the advantage of not being stored within the vehicle. This increases the power-to-weight and power-to-volume ratios. Other materials are used for special purposes, often to increase power output or to allow operation under water or in space.

* Compressed air has been commonly used in [torpedoes](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo").[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* Compressed [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"), as well as some compressed air, was used in the Japanese [Type 93 torpedo](/wiki/Type_93_torpedo "Type 93 torpedo"). Some [submarines](/wiki/Air-independent_propulsion "Air-independent propulsion") carry pure oxygen. Rockets very often use [liquid oxygen](/wiki/Liquid_oxygen "Liquid oxygen").[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* [Nitromethane](/wiki/Nitromethane "Nitromethane") is added to some racing and [model](/wiki/Model_engine "Model engine") fuels to increase power and control combustion.
* [Nitrous oxide](/wiki/Nitrous_oxide "Nitrous oxide") has been used—with extra gasoline—in tactical aircraft, and in specially equipped cars to allow short bursts of added power from engines that otherwise run on gasoline and air. It is also used in the Burt Rutan rocket spacecraft.
* [Hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide") power was under development for German World War II submarines. It may have been used in some non-nuclear submarines, and was used on some rocket engines (notably the [Black Arrow](/wiki/Black_Arrow "Black Arrow") and the [Messerschmitt Me 163](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Me_163 "Messerschmitt Me 163") rocket fighter).
* Other chemicals such as chlorine or fluorine have been used experimentally, but have not been found practical.

## Cooling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Cooling")]

Main article: [Internal combustion engine cooling](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine_cooling "Internal combustion engine cooling")

Cooling is required to remove excessive heat—high temperature can cause engine failure, usually from wear (due to high-temperature-induced failure of lubrication), cracking or warping. Two most common forms of engine cooling are [air-cooled](/wiki/Air-cooled_engine "Air-cooled engine") and [water-cooled](/wiki/Water-cooled#Automotive_usage "Water-cooled"). Most modern automotive engines are both water and air-cooled, as the water/liquid-coolant is carried to air-cooled fins and/or fans, whereas larger engines may be singularly water-cooled as they are stationary and have a constant supply of water through water-mains or fresh-water, while most power tool engines and other small engines are air-cooled. Some engines (air or water-cooled) also have an [oil cooler](/wiki/Oil_cooling "Oil cooling"). In some engines, especially for [turbine engine blade cooling](/wiki/Turbine_blade#Cooling "Turbine blade") and [liquid rocket engine cooling](/wiki/Regenerative_cooling_(rocket) "Regenerative cooling (rocket)"), fuel is used as a coolant, as it is simultaneously preheated before injecting it into a combustion chamber.

## Starting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Starting")]

Main article: [Starter motor](/wiki/Starter_motor "Starter motor")

Hand-cranking a boat diesel motor in [Inle Lake](/wiki/Inle_Lake "Inle Lake") ([Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Automobile_starter.JPG/250px-Automobile_starter.JPG)](/wiki/File:Automobile_starter.JPG)

Electric starter as used in automobiles

Internal combustion engines must have their cycles started. In reciprocating engines this is accomplished by turning the crankshaft (Wankel Rotor Shaft) which induces the cycles of intake, compression, combustion, and exhaust. The first engines were started with a turn of their [flywheels](/wiki/Flywheel "Flywheel"), while the first vehicle (the Daimler Reitwagen) was started with a hand crank. All ICE engined automobiles were started with hand cranks until [Charles Kettering](/wiki/Charles_Kettering "Charles Kettering") developed the electric starter for automobiles.[[51]](#cite_note-51) This method is now the most widely used, even among non-automobiles.

As diesel engines have become larger and their mechanisms heavier, [air starters](/wiki/Air-start_system "Air-start system") have come into use.[[52]](#cite_note-52) This is due to the lack of torque in electric starters. Air starters work by pumping compressed air into the cylinders of an engine to start it turning.

Two-wheeled vehicles may have their engines started in one of four ways:

* By pedaling, as on a bicycle
* By pushing the vehicle and then engaging the clutch, known as "run-and-bump starting"
* By kicking downward on a single pedal, known as "kick starting"
* By an electric starter, as in cars

There are also starters where a spring is compressed by a crank motion and then used to start an engine.

Some small engines use a pull-rope mechanism called "recoil starting", as the rope rewinds itself after it has been pulled out to start the engine. This method is commonly used in pushed lawn mowers and other settings where only a small amount of torque is needed to turn an engine over.

Turbine engines are frequently started by an electric motor or by compressed air.

## Measures of engine performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Measures of engine performance")]

Engine types vary greatly in a number of different ways:

* [energy efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency")
* fuel/propellant consumption ([brake specific fuel consumption](/wiki/Brake_specific_fuel_consumption "Brake specific fuel consumption") for shaft engines, [thrust specific fuel consumption](/wiki/Thrust_specific_fuel_consumption "Thrust specific fuel consumption") for jet engines)
* [power-to-weight ratio](/wiki/Power-to-weight_ratio "Power-to-weight ratio")
* [thrust to weight ratio](/wiki/Thrust_to_weight_ratio "Thrust to weight ratio")
* [torque curves](/wiki/Machine_torque "Machine torque") (for shaft engines), [thrust lapse](/wiki/Thrust_lapse "Thrust lapse") (jet engines)
* [compression ratio](/wiki/Compression_ratio "Compression ratio") for piston engines, [overall pressure ratio](/wiki/Overall_pressure_ratio "Overall pressure ratio") for jet engines and gas turbines

### Energy efficiency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Energy efficiency")]

Once ignited and burnt, the [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") products—hot gases—have more available [thermal energy](/wiki/Thermal_energy "Thermal energy") than the original compressed fuel-air mixture (which had higher [chemical energy](/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy")). This available energy is manifested as a higher [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") and [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure") that can be converted into [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") by the engine. In a reciprocating engine, the high-pressure gases inside the cylinders drive the engine's pistons.

Once the available energy has been removed, the remaining hot gases are [vented](/wiki/Exhaust_gas "Exhaust gas") (often by opening a [valve](/wiki/Poppet_valve "Poppet valve") or exposing the exhaust outlet) and this allows the piston to return to its previous position (top dead center, or TDC). The piston can then proceed to the next phase of its cycle, which varies between engines. Any thermal energy that is not translated into work is normally considered a waste product and is removed from the engine either by an air or liquid cooling system.

Internal combustion engines are considered [heat engines](/wiki/Heat_engine "Heat engine") (since the release of chemical energy in combustion has the same effect as [heat](/wiki/Heat "Heat") transfer into the engine) and as such their theoretical efficiency can be approximated by idealized [thermodynamic cycles](/wiki/Thermodynamic_cycle "Thermodynamic cycle"). The thermal efficiency of a theoretical cycle cannot exceed that of the [Carnot cycle](/wiki/Carnot_cycle "Carnot cycle"), whose efficiency is determined by the difference between the lower and upper [operating temperatures](/wiki/Operating_temperature "Operating temperature") of the engine. The upper operating temperature of an engine is limited by two main factors; the thermal operating limits of the materials, and the auto-ignition resistance of the fuel. All [metals](/wiki/Metal "Metal") and [alloys](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") have a thermal operating limit, and there is significant research into [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") materials that can be made with greater thermal stability and desirable structural properties. Higher thermal stability allows for a greater temperature difference between the lower (ambient) and upper operating temperatures, hence greater thermodynamic efficiency. Also, as the cylinder temperature rises, the fuel becomes more prone to auto-ignition. This is caused when the cylinder temperature nears the flash point of the charge. At this point, ignition can spontaneously occur before the spark plug fires, causing excessive cylinder pressures. Auto-ignition can be mitigated by using fuels with high auto-ignition resistance ([octane rating](/wiki/Octane_rating "Octane rating")), however it still puts an upper bound on the allowable peak cylinder temperature.

The [thermodynamic limits](/wiki/Thermodynamic_limit "Thermodynamic limit") assume that the engine is operating under ideal conditions: a frictionless world, ideal gases, perfect insulators, and operation for infinite time. Real world applications introduce complexities that reduce efficiency. For example, a real engine runs best at a specific load, termed its [power band](/wiki/Power_band "Power band"). The engine in a car cruising on a highway is usually operating significantly below its ideal load, because it is designed for the higher loads required for rapid acceleration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In addition, factors such as [wind resistance](/wiki/Wind_resistance "Wind resistance") reduce overall system efficiency. Vehicle [fuel economy](/wiki/Fuel_economy_in_automobiles "Fuel economy in automobiles") is measured in [miles per gallon](/wiki/Miles_per_gallon "Miles per gallon") or in liters per 100 kilometers. The volume of hydrocarbon assumes a standard energy content.

Even when aided with turbochargers and stock efficiency aids, most engines retain an *average* efficiency of about 18–20%.[[53]](#cite_note-53) However, the latest technologies in [Formula One engines](/wiki/Formula_One_engines "Formula One engines") have seen a boost in thermal efficiency past 50%.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
There are many inventions aimed at increasing the efficiency of IC engines. In general, practical engines are always compromised by trade-offs between different properties such as efficiency, weight, power, heat, response, exhaust emissions, or noise. Sometimes economy also plays a role in the cost to manufacture an engine, and to manufacture and distribute the fuel. Increasing engine efficiency brings better fuel economy, but only if the fuel cost per energy content is the same.

### Measures of fuel efficiency and propellant efficiency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Measures of fuel efficiency and propellant efficiency")]

For stationary and shaft engines including propeller engines, fuel consumption is measured by calculating the [brake specific fuel consumption](/wiki/Brake_specific_fuel_consumption "Brake specific fuel consumption"), which measures the mass flow rate of fuel consumption divided by the power produced.

For internal combustion engines in the form of jet engines, the power output varies drastically with airspeed and a less variable measure is used: [thrust specific fuel consumption](/wiki/Thrust_specific_fuel_consumption "Thrust specific fuel consumption") (TSFC), which is the mass of propellant needed to generate [impulses](/wiki/Impulse_(physics) "Impulse (physics)") that is measured in either pound force-hour or the grams of propellant needed to generate an impulse that measures one kilonewton-second.

For rockets, TSFC can be used, but typically other equivalent measures are traditionally used, such as [specific impulse](/wiki/Specific_impulse "Specific impulse") and [effective exhaust velocity](/wiki/Effective_exhaust_velocity "Effective exhaust velocity").

### Air pollution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Air pollution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Resort_Svat%C3%A1_Kate%C5%99ina%2C_z%C3%A1kaz_vjezdu.jpg/250px-Resort_Svat%C3%A1_Kate%C5%99ina%2C_z%C3%A1kaz_vjezdu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Resort_Svat%C3%A1_Kate%C5%99ina,_z%C3%A1kaz_vjezdu.jpg)

Internal combustion engines such as reciprocating internal combustion engines produce [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") emissions, due to incomplete combustion of [carbonaceous](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") fuel. The main derivatives of the process are [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") CO  
2, water and some [soot](/wiki/Soot "Soot")—also called [particulate matter](/wiki/Atmospheric_particulate_matter "Atmospheric particulate matter") (PM).[[55]](#cite_note-55) The effects of inhaling particulate matter have been studied in humans and animals and include asthma, lung cancer, cardiovascular issues, and premature death.[[56]](#cite_note-56) There are, however, some additional products of the combustion process that include [nitrogen oxides](/wiki/Nitrogen_oxide "Nitrogen oxide") and [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") and some uncombusted hydrocarbons, depending on the operating conditions and the fuel-air ratio.

Carbon dioxide emissions from internal combustion engines (particularly ones using [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") such as gasoline and diesel) contribute to human-induced [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). Increasing the engine's [fuel efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency") can reduce, but not eliminate, the amount of CO  
2 emissions as carbon-based fuel combustion produces CO  
2. Since removing CO  
2 from engine exhaust is impractical, there is increasing interest in alternatives. Sustainable fuels such as [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"), [synfuels](/wiki/Synfuel "Synfuel"), and [electric motors](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor") powered by batteries are examples.

Not all of the fuel is completely consumed by the combustion process. A small amount of fuel is present after combustion, and some of it reacts to form oxygenates, such as [formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde") or [acetaldehyde](/wiki/Acetaldehyde "Acetaldehyde"), or hydrocarbons not originally present in the input fuel mixture. Incomplete combustion usually results from insufficient [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") to achieve the perfect [stoichiometric](/wiki/Stoichiometric "Stoichiometric") ratio. The flame is "quenched" by the relatively cool cylinder walls, leaving behind unreacted fuel that is expelled with the exhaust. When running at lower speeds, quenching is commonly observed in diesel (compression ignition) engines that run on natural gas. Quenching reduces efficiency and increases knocking, sometimes causing the engine to stall. Incomplete combustion also leads to the production of [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") (CO). Further chemicals released are [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene") and [1,3-butadiene](/wiki/1,3-butadiene "1,3-butadiene") that are also [hazardous air pollutants](/wiki/Hazardous_air_pollutant "Hazardous air pollutant").

Increasing the amount of air in the engine reduces emissions of incomplete combustion products, but also promotes reaction between oxygen and [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") in the air to produce [nitrogen oxides](/wiki/Nitrogen_oxide "Nitrogen oxide") (NO*x*). NO*x* is hazardous to both plant and animal health, and leads to the production of [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone") (O  
3). Ozone is not emitted directly; rather, it is a secondary air pollutant, produced in the atmosphere by the reaction of NO*x* and [volatile organic compounds](/wiki/Volatile_organic_compound "Volatile organic compound") in the presence of sunlight. Ground-level ozone is harmful to human health and the environment. Though the same chemical substance, ground-level ozone should not be confused with [stratospheric ozone](/wiki/Stratospheric_ozone "Stratospheric ozone"), or the [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer"), which protects the earth from harmful ultraviolet rays.

Carbon fuels containing sulfur produce [sulfur monoxides](/wiki/Sulfur_monoxide "Sulfur monoxide") (SO) and [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") (SO  
2) contributing to [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain").

In the United States, nitrogen oxides, [PM](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates"), carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide, and ozone, are regulated as [criteria air pollutants](/wiki/Criteria_air_pollutant "Criteria air pollutant") under the [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)") to levels where human health and welfare are protected. Other pollutants, such as benzene and 1,3-butadiene, are regulated as [hazardous air pollutants](/wiki/Hazardous_air_pollutant "Hazardous air pollutant") whose emissions must be lowered as much as possible depending on technological and practical considerations.

NO*x*, carbon monoxide and other pollutants are frequently controlled via [exhaust gas recirculation](/wiki/Exhaust_gas_recirculation "Exhaust gas recirculation") which returns some of the exhaust back into the engine intake. [Catalytic converters](/wiki/Catalytic_converter "Catalytic converter") are used to convert exhaust chemicals to CO  
2 (a [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas")), H  
2O (water vapour, also a greenhouse gas) and N  
2 (nitrogen).

See also: [Air suction valve](/wiki/Air_suction_valve "Air suction valve")

#### Non-road engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Non-road engines")]

Main article: [Non-road engine § Emission standards](/wiki/Non-road_engine#Emission_standards "Non-road engine")

The emission standards used by many countries have special requirements for [non-road engines](/wiki/Non-road_engine "Non-road engine") which are used by equipment and vehicles that are not operated on the public roadways. The standards are separated from the road vehicles.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Noise pollution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Noise pollution")]

Significant contributions to [noise pollution](/wiki/Noise_pollution "Noise pollution") are made by internal combustion engines. Automobile and truck traffic operating on highways and street systems produce noise, as do aircraft flights due to jet noise, particularly supersonic-capable aircraft. Rocket engines create the most intense noise.

### Idling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Idling")]

Main article: [Idle reduction](/wiki/Idle_reduction "Idle reduction")

Internal combustion engines continue to consume fuel and emit pollutants while idling. Idling is reduced by [stop-start systems](/wiki/Start-stop_system "Start-stop system").

## Carbon dioxide formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Carbon dioxide formation")]

A good way to estimate the mass of carbon dioxide that is released when one litre of diesel fuel (or gasoline) is combusted can be found as follows:[[58]](#cite_note-58)

As a good approximation the chemical formula of diesel is C  
nH  
2n. In reality diesel is a mixture of different molecules. As carbon has a molar mass of 12 g/mol and hydrogen (atomic) has a molar mass of about 1 g/mol, the fraction by weight of carbon in diesel is roughly 12⁄14.

The reaction of diesel combustion is given by:

2C  
nH  
2n + 3nO  
2 ⇌ 2nCO  
2 + 2nH  
2O

Carbon dioxide has a molar mass of 44 g/mol as it consists of 2 atoms of oxygen (16 g/mol) and 1 atom of carbon (12 g/mol). So 12 g of carbon yields 44 g of carbon dioxide.

Diesel has a density of 0.838 kg per litre.

Putting everything together the mass of carbon dioxide that is produced by burning 1 litre of diesel can be calculated as:

![{\displaystyle 0.838kg/L\cdot {\frac {12}{14}}\cdot {\frac {44}{12}}=2.63kg/L}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3064e4a5bfdebe0803e0ac221ada35f60de1a28b)

The figure obtained with this estimation is close to the values found in the literature.

For gasoline, with a density of 0.75 kg/L and a ratio of carbon to hydrogen atoms of about 6 to 14, the estimated value of carbon dioxide emission from burning 1 litre of gasoline is:

![{\displaystyle 0.75kg/L\cdot {{\frac {6\cdot 12}{6\cdot 12+14}}\cdot 1}\cdot {\frac {44}{12}}=2.3kg/L}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7b079ee4a909756eea96091bee84088eec4a79e3)

## Parasitic loss

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Parasitic loss")]

The term *parasitic loss* is often applied to devices that take energy from the engine in order to enhance the engine's ability to create more energy or convert energy to motion. In the internal combustion engine, almost every mechanical component, including the [drivetrain](/wiki/Drivetrain "Drivetrain"), causes parasitic loss and could thus be characterized as a parasitic load.

### Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Examples")]

[Bearings](/wiki/Bearing_(mechanical) "Bearing (mechanical)"), oil pumps, [piston rings](/wiki/Piston_ring "Piston ring"), valve springs, [flywheels](/wiki/Flywheel "Flywheel"), [transmissions](/wiki/Transmission_(mechanics) "Transmission (mechanics)"), [driveshafts](/wiki/Driveshaft "Driveshaft"), and [differentials](/wiki/Differential_(mechanics) "Differential (mechanics)") all act as parasitic loads that rob the system of power. These parasitic loads can be divided into two categories: those inherent to the working of the engine and those drivetrain losses incurred in the systems that transfer power from the engine to the road (such as the transmission, driveshaft, differentials and axles).

For example, the former category (engine parasitic loads) includes the oil pump used to lubricate the engine, which is a necessary parasite that consumes power from the engine (its host). Another example of an engine parasitic load is a [supercharger](/wiki/Supercharger "Supercharger"), which derives its power from the engine and creates more power for the engine. The power that the supercharger consumes is parasitic loss and is usually expressed in [kilowatt](/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt") or [horsepower](/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower"). While the power that the supercharger consumes in comparison to what it generates is small, it is still measurable or calculable. One of the desirable features of a [turbocharger](/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger") over a supercharger is the lower parasitic loss of the former.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Drivetrain parasitic losses include both steady state and dynamic loads. Steady state loads occur at constant speeds and may originate in discrete components such as the [torque converter](/wiki/Torque_converter "Torque converter"), the transmission [oil pump](/wiki/Oil_pump_(internal_combustion_engine) "Oil pump (internal combustion engine)"), and/or [clutch](/wiki/Clutch "Clutch") drag, and in seal/bearing drag, churning of lubricant and gear [windage](/wiki/Windage "Windage")/[friction](/wiki/Friction "Friction") found throughout the system. Dynamic loads occur under acceleration and are caused by [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia") of rotating components and/or increased friction.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-60)

### Measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Measurement")]

While rules of thumb such as a 15% power loss from drivetrain parasitic loads have been commonly repeated, the actual loss of energy due to parasitic loads varies between systems. It can be influenced by powertrain design, lubricant type and temperature and many other factors.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) In automobiles, drivetrain loss can be quantified by measuring the difference between power measured by an [engine dynamometer](/wiki/Engine_test_stand "Engine test stand") and a [chassis dynamometer](/wiki/Chassis_dynamometer "Chassis dynamometer"). However, this method is primarily useful for measuring steady state loads and may not accurately reflect losses due to dynamic loads.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-60) More advanced methods can be used in a laboratory setting, such as measuring in-cylinder pressure measurements, flow rate and temperature at certain points, and testing of individual parts or sub-assemblies to determine friction and pumping losses.[[62]](#cite_note-:2-62)

For example, in a dynamometer test by [*Hot Rod* magazine](/wiki/Hot_Rod_(magazine) "Hot Rod (magazine)"), a [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") equipped with a modified 357ci [small-block Ford V8](/wiki/Ford_small_block_engine "Ford small block engine") engine and an automatic transmission had a measured drivetrain power loss averaging 33%. In the same test, a [Buick](/wiki/Buick "Buick") equipped with a modified 455ci V8 engine and a 4-speed manual transmission was measured to have an average drivetrain power loss of 21%.[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63)

Laboratory testing of a heavy-duty diesel engine determined that 1.3% of the fuel energy input was lost to parasitic loads of engine accessories such as water and oil pumps.[[62]](#cite_note-:2-62)

### Reduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Internal_combustion_engine&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Reduction")]

Automotive engineers and tuners commonly make design choices that reduce parasitic loads in order to improve efficiency and power output. These may involve the choice of major engine components or systems, such as the use of [dry sump](/wiki/Dry_sump "Dry sump") lubrication system over a [wet sump](/wiki/Wet_sump "Wet sump") system. Alternately, this can be effected through substitution of minor components available as aftermarket modifications, such as exchanging a directly engine-driven fan for one equipped with a [fan clutch](/wiki/Fan_clutch "Fan clutch") or an electric fan.[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63) Another modification to reduce parasitic loss, usually seen in track-only cars, is the replacement of an engine-driven water pump for an electrical water pump.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The reduction in parasitic loss from these changes may be due to reduced friction or many other variables that cause the design to be more efficient.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
